problem_summary: 2018 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability): build a model that measures a state's fragility, isolates how climate change drives fragility (directly and indirectly), classifies states as fragile/vulnerable/stable, predicts tipping points, evaluates intervention policies and their cost, and scales the model up/down to continents and cities. Team 73119's answer centers on a custom composite fragility index (FI) called PSA, validated against the Fragile States Index (FSI), plus grey-model forecasting and regression-based causal chains.
models_used: PSA (Pressure-Sensitivity-Adaptability) composite index: a custom 3-dimension extension of the established PSR (Pressure-State-Response) environmental model, with FI = theta1*P + theta2*S + theta3*A built over a 4-layer Target/Dimension/Theme/Indicator hierarchy of 16 indicators
AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process): pairwise-comparison score matrices to assign subjective weights to indicators within each dimension
EWM (Entropy Weight Method): data-driven objective weighting from indicator entropy across 20 countries, used to offset AHP subjectivity
Combined weighting: final weight Wi = 0.8*W_AHP + 0.2*W_EWM (a convex blend of subjective and objective weights)
Min-max normalization with three indicator polarities: positive (+, bigger-better), negative (-, smaller-better), and a special temperature-anomaly form r = 1 - |ta|/|ta|_max

Multiple linear regression: fits functional relationships linking climate indicators to non-climatic indicators (e.g., Water = 1.099t + 42.15; CropProduction = 3.551t + 114; HumanRight and DemocracyRate as functions of elevation) to model indirect climate impact
GM(1,1) Grey Prediction Model: used twice — to forecast Bangladesh's FI trajectory to the 2076 tipping point (FI=0.6), and to project intervention budgets at 5/10/20-year horizons
Counterfactual / scenario analysis: 'FI without climate change' built by holding climate indicators fixed at 2006 values and propagating through the regression equations + PSA
Threshold classifier: FI>=0.6 Stable, 0.4<=FI<0.6 Vulnerable, 0<=FI<0.4 Fragile
SPSS multiple imputation for missing indicator values
key_techniques: Anchoring a novel index on a recognized framework (PSR -> PSA) so the model reads as a principled extension rather than an ad hoc score
Hybrid subjective+objective weighting (AHP+EWM) explicitly framed as each method covering the other's weakness — a strong, defensible answer to 'why these weights?'
External validation against ground truth: comparing PSA output to FSI rankings/classifications across 20 countries (Appendix A table + scatterplot), turning a subjective index into a falsifiable one

Separating direct vs indirect climate effects via a causal influence diagram (Figure 3) plus regression equations that quantify the indirect pathways — directly answering the problem's hardest sub-question
Counterfactual construction (hold climate constant at 2006, regenerate FI) to literally show the climate-attributable gap (Figure 6, DRC)
Tying model dips to real documented events (2007 Cyclone Sidr, 2008 Congo cholera, 2012 droughts/cold waves) to ground-truth the index against history
Mapping the six real Bangladesh government interventions onto specific model indicators, then costing them with GM(1,1) to produce concrete 5/10/20-year budget numbers
Portability demonstrated concretely: continents via averaging representative countries; cities via indicator substitution (forest cover -> greenland rate, crop index -> food safety index)
Sensitivity analysis on the weighting blend parameter theta1 (0.2-0.8) showing the FI trend is stable
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it does the four things O-award E papers must do, and does them in order. (1) It directly and completely answers every task — fragility measurement, the fragile/vulnerable/stable trichotomy, the direct-vs-indirect climate decomposition, tipping-point prediction, policy+cost, and continent/city scaling — with no task skipped. (2) It defends its modeling choices: the PSR->PSA lineage, the AHP+EWM blend with an explicit rationale, and a sensitivity check all preempt the 'arbitrary index' criticism that sinks most fragility papers. (3) It validates against external ground truth (FSI) rather than just asserting plausibility, which is rare and persuasive. (4) It is concrete — real countries (DRC, Bangladesh), real historical disasters tied to curve features, real government interventions, and real budget tables producing actual forecast numbers (2076 tipping point; 35%/5yr intervention growth). The causal influence diagram + regression equations for indirect effects is exactly the conceptual move the problem rewards. The combination of a defensible composite, ground-truth validation, and end-to-end concreteness is what separates O from finalist."
section_highlights: "Strong structure: Summary Sheet front-loads the whole arc (PSA, AHP+EWM, FSI validation, DRC counterfactual, BD GM(1,1) -> 2076, intervention costing, continent portability, sensitivity) so a judge sees the complete story in one page. Clear task-to-section mapping (each subsection explicitly cites 'Refer to Task N'). Section 3.2 walks the index build as a numbered pipeline (identify -> impute -> normalize -> AHP -> EWM -> weighted average), which is easy to follow and reproduce. Section 3.3 pairs a visual influence diagram with a regression table and prose splitting direct vs indirect channels — the conceptual centerpiece, well placed. Section 3.4 (Test PSA) puts validation right after model construction, building credibility before the case studies. Case studies (DRC counterfactual chart; BD prediction + intervention cost tables) deliver concrete deliverables. Honest Strengths/Weaknesses section. Appendices carry the supporting FI/FSI comparison table and Bangladesh budget data."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Mathematical presentation is sloppy/garbled: the normalization equations (1)-(3), the EWM proportion eq (8), and the entropy formula (eq 9 appears twice with inconsistent forms) are typographically broken and not self-consistent — a real judge would dock rigor even if the intent is recoverable
AHP is asserted, not shown: no pairwise comparison matrices, no consistency-ratio (CR) check are reported, so the subjective weights are unauditable
The 0.8/0.2 AHP/EWM blend is arbitrary; sensitivity analysis varies it but never justifies why 0.8 is the chosen operating point
GM(1,1) forecasting 80 years out (to 2076 and 2085) is a severe extrapolation — grey models are only credible for short horizons; the smooth straight-line prediction curve is implausibly clean
Regression relationships (e.g., Water=1.099t+42.15) are reported with no R^2, sample size, p-values, or scatter — likely overfit on a single country's ~10 data points
Continent FI by simple averaging of a few 'representative' countries is crude and acknowledged as a weakness; selection of representatives is unexplained and could bias results

The FI orientation is counterintuitive (higher FI = MORE stable per Appendix A: NZ=0.847, DRC=0.337) yet the name 'Fragile/Fragility Index' and parts of the prose imply higher=more fragile — internal inconsistency a judge would notice
Assumptions ('countries are stable', riots/wars excluded) conveniently remove the very instability the problem is about; the model self-admittedly fails for states in large-scale war
Validation is correlational eyeballing of trend agreement (Figure 4 scatter), not a quantified fit metric (no Spearman rank correlation reported despite having the data to compute it)
Small evidence base: 20 countries for EWM weighting and validation is thin for a global fragility claim
Some English-language and citation artifacts (broken reference [], garbled symbols) suggest time pressure and weaken polish
reusable_patterns: Extend-a-known-framework move: take an established model (PSR, SIR, gravity model, etc.) and rename/extend it into a custom acronym (PSA) — instant credibility plus novelty for index-style problems

Hybrid AHP+EWM weighting with a convex blend Wi = a*W_AHP + b*W_EWM is a reusable, defensible recipe for ANY multi-indicator composite-index problem, and it auto-generates a sensitivity-analysis knob (vary a)
Four-layer indicator hierarchy (Target -> Dimension -> Theme -> Indicator) with explicit +/-/* polarity tags is a clean, judge-friendly way to present and normalize heterogeneous indicators
Validate-against-ground-truth pattern: whenever a public benchmark exists (here FSI), compare your index's ranking/classification to it — converts a subjective score into a falsifiable, defended one
Direct-vs-indirect decomposition via influence diagram + regression equations: a transferable template for any 'how does X affect Y directly and indirectly' sub-question

Counterfactual-by-freezing: build the 'without intervention/without driver' baseline by holding the driver variables fixed at a base year and propagating through fitted relationships — clean way to attribute effect size
Map real-world policies onto model indicators, then cost them with a trend forecaster (GM(1,1)) to produce concrete budget deliverables — strong for policy/cost tasks

Portability via indicator substitution: when scaling to a different unit (city vs country), swap unavailable indicators for available proxies (forest cover -> greenland rate) rather than rebuilding the model
Tie model-curve features to documented real events to ground-truth a time series qualitatively — cheap, persuasive credibility booster
Front-load the entire solution arc in the Summary Sheet and tag every subsection to its Task — maximizes judge readability under fast skim conditions
